coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots encapsulated tremella-like NiCo2O4 for advanced asymmetric supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Jiahuan
Wang, Jing
Liu, Sen
Wu, Weiming
Jia, Taixuan
Yang, Ze
Mu, Shichun
Huang, Yunhui
coredata.dc:description: A tremella-like NiCo2O4@graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite is prepared where the tremella-like NiCo2O4 is homogeneously encapsulated by GQDs. Herein, the GQDs based on π-conjugated core with abundant edge sites, make NiCo2O4 favorable for advanced electric energy storage (EES). In terms of the synergistic effect between the conductive GQDs and tremella-like NiCo2O4, the as prepared NiCo2O4@GQDs composite exhibits an excellent specific capacitance of 1242 F g−1 at 30 A g−1 in 2.0 M KOH electrolytes, much higher than that of pristine NiCo2O4 (790 F g−1 at 30 A g−1). Also, it manifests outstanding cycle stability (99% capacitance retention after 4000 cycles). Furthermore, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) is assembled by pairing NiCo2O4@GQDs composite as positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as negative electrode (NiCo2O4@GQDs//AC), and its specific energy density is as high as 38 W h kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mixed transition metal oxides
Tremella-like NiCo2O4
Graphene quantum dots
Pseudo-capacitors
Asymmetric supercapacitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns (a) and Raman spectra (b) of NiCo2O4@GQDs and NiCo2O4. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) SEM, (b) TEM, (c) HRTEM images for the mesoporous flower-like NiCo2O4. (d) SEM, (e) TEM, (f) HRTEM images for NiCo2O4@GQDs. (g–k) TEM image and the corresponding elemental mapping of NiCo2O4@GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical characteristics of the NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs composite (a) Cyclic voltammetry (CV) at same scan rate. (b) Galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves at current densities of 1 A g−1. (c) Specific capacitance at various current densities. (d) Nyquist plots fitted with the corresponding equivalent circuit (inset is the equivalent circuit diagrams). (e) Cycling performances. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Electrochemical characteristics of the activated carbon (AC) (a) CV at various scan rates. (b) GCD curves at various current densities (c) Specific capacitance. (d) Nyquist impedance spectra. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Electrochemical characteristics of the NiCo2O4@GQDs//AC asymmetric supercapacitor. (b) CV curves at different scan rates (5, 10 and 20 mVs−1) in 2 M KOH GCD curves at various current densities in the potential window of 0–1.6 V. (c) Specific capacitance. (d) Nyquist impedance spectra. (e) Ragone plots of energy density and power density of different materials. (f) Cycling performance at a scan rate of 4 A g−1 for 3000 cycles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration of the preparation progress of the NiCo2O4 (a) and NiCo2O4@GQDs (b).

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots encapsulated tremella-like NiCo2O4 for advanced asymmetric supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering, Anyang Institute of Technology, Anyang, 455000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and TcSUH, University of Houston, Houston, 77204, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan, Hubei, 430070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Advanced Technology for Materials Synthesis and Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Material Processing and Die & Mould Technology
School of Materials Science and Engineering
Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A tremella-like NiCo2O4@graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite is prepared where the tremella-like NiCo2O4 is homogeneously encapsulated by GQDs. Herein, the GQDs based on π-conjugated core with abundant edge sites, make NiCo2O4 favorable for advanced electric energy storage (EES). In terms of the synergistic effect between the conductive GQDs and tremella-like NiCo2O4, the as prepared NiCo2O4@GQDs composite exhibits an excellent specific capacitance of 1242 F g−1 at 30 A g−1 in 2.0 M KOH electrolytes, much higher than that of pristine NiCo2O4 (790 F g−1 at 30 A g−1). Also, it manifests outstanding cycle stability (99% capacitance retention after 4000 cycles). Furthermore, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) is assembled by pairing NiCo2O4@GQDs composite as positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as negative electrode (NiCo2O4@GQDs//AC), and its specific energy density is as high as 38 W h kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors are one of the most promising candidates in electric energy storage systems owing to its high-power density, fast charge/discharge performance, long lifetime and low maintenance cost [1–7]. In addition, they also have been considered as one of special storage devices dedicating to alleviate the energy crisis and meet the ever-growing consumption demand. The pseudo-capacitor, as one of the supercapacitors, undergoes reversible Faradaic reactions that occurs in the electro-active species, such as various metal oxides [8], metal hydroxides [9] and carbonates [10]. However, the intrinsic poor energy density of widely used mono-metal oxides seriously limits their electrochemical performance by hindering the transfer of electrons. Other prevented factors for its widespread applications are the toxic nature and high price (such as the high-cost RuO2) [7]. Therefore, it is essential to construct low-cost and environmentally friendly electrode materials with excellent electrochemical performance.

Among all EES materials reported previously, the mixed transition metal oxides (MTMOs) [11–30], like NiCo2O4, have been recommended as superior electrode materials for supercapacitors, because of their low cost and high electrochemical activity when compared to mono-metal oxides (NiO and Co3O4). But MTMOs often show short cycle lifetime on account of some natural insufficiencies such as poor rate performances, low electronic conductivities and volume change effect.

To overcome these shortcomings for MTMOs, some carbon materials have been employed to increase the surface wettability, conductivity and surface area [31]. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are of single or few layer graphenes with a tiny size of only several nanometers. GQDs as a new type of quantum dots (QDs), possess numerous novel chemical and physical properties of both graphene and QDs [32,33]. Due to their unique properties, GQDs have demonstrated great potential for various applications, such as photovoltaic devices, bio-imaging devices, light-emitting diodes, catalysts, sensors, fuel cells and lithium ion batteries. Up to now, several pioneer researches have displayed the promising future of GQDs as electrode materials for supercapacitors [34–37]. More recently, Zhang and co-worker [38] reported a work about ultramicroporous carbons puzzled by GQDs to enhance electrical performance of carbon. It is found that GQDs can provide an outstanding interface for the interactions between electrodes and electrolytes, improving the electrochemical performances including specific capacitance, rate performance, and cycling stability.

The “bottom-up” method is traditional and widely used for the composite of metal oxides and graphene to increase the conductivity of metal oxides [9,11,16,17]. However, this method cannot well regulate morphology and activate electrochemical performance of metal oxides. GQDs with a small size have similar conductivity to graphene [38], so the “top-down” method can be applied to control the morphology of metal oxides with GQDs encapsulation. For example, Fan group reported a GQDs encapsulated VO2 composite prepared by electrochemical deposition [39], showing excellent Li/Na storage performance. However, the electrochemical deposition method is not conducive to large-scale applications. Thus, in this study, we achieve GQDs decorated tremella-like NiCo2O4 by a solvent evaporation guide GQDs self-assembly way, as shown in Scheme 1 . This method not only greatly improves the electrochemical properties of NiCo2O4, but also provides a novel idea for the composite of GQDs and metal compounds. Firstly, the NiCo2O4 was synthesized by a solvothermal method. After that, the tremella-like NiCo2O4 and GQDs were composited to get NiCo2O4@GQDs. The as-obtained NiCo2O4@GQDs composite manifests a remarkably high specific capacitance and rate performance which are far beyond that of NiCo2O4. Also, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was fabricated by exploiting NiCo2O4@GQDs as the positive electrode and activated carbon (AC) as the negative electrode. The as fabricated ASC shows a specific capacitance of 107.3 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, corresponding to a high energy density of 38.15 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1. Moreover, it even exhibits high capacity retention after long cycles at a large current density.

Cobalt nitrate [Co(NO3)2·6H2O], nickel nitrate [Co(NO3)2·6H2O], KOH, graphite, N, N′-dimethylformamide (DMF), 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) were bought from China National Medicines Company. Ketjen Black carbon and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) from the other commercial ways. All raw materials used in the experiments are analytical grade and without further treatment.

All the chemical reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification. The tremella-like NiCo2O4 was prepared by a hydrothermal method followed by thermal annealing treatment. Typically, 2 mmol Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 1 mmol Ni(NO3)2·6H2O in 80 mL water/1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidinone (NMP) mixture (Vwater/VNMP = 1:1) under stirring for 20 min to form a clear pink solution. The as prepared solution was then sealed in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the final sample was washed with deionized water and ethanol for several times and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h. Finally, the samples were annealed at 300 °C in an argon atmosphere for 6 h to obtain the tremella-like NiCo2O4.

At first, graphite oxide (GO) was prepared by a modified Hummers method [36]. GQDs were synthesized from GO powder by one-step process. The typical procedures are briefly described as follows. GO (540 mg) was suspended in DMF (40 mL) under ultrasonication (120 W, 40 kHz) for 30 min, and then the suspension was transferred into a poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (60 mL) and heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After reactor was cooled to room temperature naturally, the product was filtered by using a 220 nm microporous membrane. The brown filter solution was collected, which is the GQDs dispersed in DMF.

For comparison, NiCo2O4@GQDs samples were prepared from the NiCo2O4 precursors. The NiCo2O4 precursors were dispersed in GQDs solution with mass ratio of nearly 30:1, by magnetic stirring for 30 min, and then dried at 60 °C for 24 h. Finally, the NiCo2O4 precursors@GQDs were annealed in an argon atmosphere at 300 °C for 6 h to obtain the tremella-like NiCo2O4@GQDs.

The electrochemical properties were tested using a three-electrode cell in a solution of 2 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. Platinum sheet and Ag/AgCl electrode were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The working electrodes were made by coating a slurry containing active material, Ketjen black carbon and polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) binder in a mass ratio of 80:10:10 to obtain slurries onto nickel foam (1 × 1 cm2). The obtained composite was pressed and dried at 60 °C under vacuum. The mass loading of the active material in the electrode was approximately 8 mg. The asymmetric supercapacitor was assembled in a two-electrode configuration using NiCo2O4@GQDs electrode and activated carbon (AC) as the positive and negative electrodes, respectively, in 2 M KOH aqueous electrolyte with one piece of cellulose paper as the separator.

All the electrochemical measurements were carried out on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation. The electrode mass was optimized according to report by Snook et al. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was observed on the Princeton electrochemical workstation over the frequency range of 0.01–100 kHz with an amplitude of 10 mV.

Fig. 1 a displays the XRD patterns of NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs. Two patterns have obvious diffraction peaks at 31.1°, 36.7°, 44.6°, 55.4°, 59.1°, and 64.9° which can be well identified as (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes of the spinel NiCo2O4 phase (JCPDS card no. 20-0781), respectively. Raman spectrum is effective to characterize graphene-based materials. Fig. 1b exhibits NiCo2O4@GQDs has two vibrational peaks at around 1346 cm−1 and 1594 cm−1 which can be attributed to the D and G bands, respectively. These evidences the presence of graphitic carbon, indicating a higher electronic conjugation for NiCo2O4@GQDs over NiCo2O4. The high peak intensity ratio (I
D /I
G = 1.21) ratio of D1 and G bands demonstrates the formation of more edge defects due to the introduction of GQDs. These make NiCo2O4@GQDs have a benefit to deliver better performance than NiCo2O4. Both of NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs exhibit two diagnostic peaks at 465 and 534 cm−1, corresponding to Eg and F2g modes of NiCo2O4, respectively. From the XRD and Raman results, it can be concluded that the NiCo2O4@GQDs composite can form through a simple “dipping and drying” process followed by chemical reduction.

Morphologies of the tremella-like NiCo2O4 were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a, the particles of NiCo2O4 display a tremella-like structure which could allow electrolytes to freely diffuse inside through the structures. Fig. 2b shows the typical transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the tremella-like NiCo2O4. The nanosheets of the tremella-like NiCo2O4 with a regular boundary, clearly demonstrate the spinel structure (Figs. S1a–c in the ESI†). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of NiCo2O4 further reveals its crystal structural characteristics. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.28 nm corresponds to the (220) plane of the spinel NiCo2O4 structure (Fig. 2c and Fig. S1d in the ESI†).

The typical morphology of the as-prepared GQDs is characterized by TEM (Fig. S2) with a uniform size distribution of 2–4.5 nm. HR-TEM image reveals the 0.21 nm interlayer spacing which corresponds to the (100) plane of GQDs (Fig. S2b in the ESI†). Atomic force microscope (AFM) images further elucidate highly scattered GQDs on the silicon substrate with average height of 0.35 nm, corresponding to single layer graphene (Figs. S2c and d in the ESI†).

Fig. 2d, Figs. S3a and S3b show typical SEM images of the tremella-like NiCo2O4@GQDs composite. By comparing Fig. 2a and d, it shows that the introduced GQDs do not change the morphology of NiCo2O4 significantly. The feature of NiCo2O4@GQDs composites is clearly revealed through HRTEM images (Fig. 2e and Fig. S3c in the ESI†). They indicate that there are one more lattice spacing than NiCo2O4, among which the spacing of about 0.21 nm corresponds to the (100) plane of GQDs (Fig. 2f and Fig. S3d in the ESI†). The TEM image and corresponding elemental mapping of elemental Ni, Co, O, C are shown in Fig. 2g–k, confirming GQDs are successfully coated on NiCo2O4.

As illustrated in Figs. S4a and S4b, the specific surface area and pore size of NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs were calculated by the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isothermal curves at 77 K. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area of NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs composites is 155 and 149 m2/g, respectively. The pore size distribution curves indicate the pore size range of 8–10 nm in diameter, which means both of NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs are of mesoporous structures.

The cyclic voltammetry (CV) and galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements of the as-prepared NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs electrodes were performed as a three-electrode system in 2.0 M KOH aqueous electrolytes, as shown in Fig. S5. Fig. 3 a presents their CV curves measured at the same scan rate, clearly indicating the pseudo-capacitance behaviour in the potential range of 0–0.6 V vs. SCE. Compared with the pure NiCo2O4 electrode, the NiCo2O4@GQDs electrode displays a pair of significantly enhanced redox peaks at the voltage of 0.18 V and 0.35 V, respectively, which originates from good conductivity and rapid charge spread ability within NiCo2O4@GQDs. The well-defined redox peaks come from the Faradaic redox reactions of M-O/M-O-OH associated with OH− anions, where M means Ni and Co ions. The reversible charge storage mechanism of NiCo2O4 can be elucidated as following equations:∗∗
[1] NiCo2O4 + OH¯ + H2O → NiOOH + 2CoOOH + e−
[2] CoOOH + OH¯ → CoO2 + H2O + e−

A GCD test was carried out within the voltage range of 0–0.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at the current density of 1 A g−1. As shown in Fig. 3b, the GCD curves of NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs display a plateau of pseudo-capacitive characteristics, consistent with the CV curves. Furthermore, the charge and discharge curves of such two materials at different current densities are shown in Figs. 3c and S6, respectively, further demonstrating their pseudo-capacitive behaviours. The NiCo2O4@GQDs electrode delivers specific capacitances of 1382, 1364, 1344, 1322, 1314, 1278 and 1242 F g−1 at current densities of 1, 2, 4, 5, 10, 20 and 30 A g−1, respectively. However, pure NiCo2O4 only achieves 1190, 1150, 1030, 1113, 998, 820 and 790 F g−1 at correspondent current densities. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured to investigate the stability of NiCo2O4 and NiCo2O4@GQDs, as shown in Fig. 3d. The diameter for the semicircle in the Nyquist plots at the high frequency region can represent the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of the interface between the electrode material and electrolyte. The Rct of these two electrodes were evaluated by fitting the Nyquist plots, and corresponding Nyquist plots were fitted with an equivalent circuit model exhibited in the inset of Fig. 3d. The fitted Rct values of NiCo2O4@GQDs and NiCo2O4 are 0.035 and 2.15Ω, respectively. This further indicates the superior electrochemical activity of NiCo2O4@GQDs in comparison with that of NiCo2O4.

Fig. 3e shows the longtime cycling stability of the two materials. During the first 400 cycles, the specific capacitance of the composite increases gradually from 1382 to 1402 F g−1, which can be assigned to the activation processing for NiCo2O4. The NiCo2O4@GQDs electrode presents a maximum specific capacitance of 1382 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, with good retention of nearly 99% over 4000 cycles. This suggests that outstanding cycle stability makes NiCo2O4@GQDs more suitable for practical applications in electrochemical energy storage fields. In contrast, pristine NiCo2O4 only possesses relatively poor electrochemical performance with a maximum specific capacitance of 1190 F g−1 at 1 A g−1, even a good capacitance retention of nearly 99% can be achieved after 4000 cycles.

To validate the practical application of tremella-like NiCo2O4@GQDs, an asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) was made-up by pairing with an activated carbon (AC) negative electrode on the substrate of nickel foam. The structure and morphology of AC were observed by SEM, as presented in Fig. S7. Fig. 4 a shows CV curves of the negative electrode at scan rates of 5, 10, and 20 mV s−1 between −1.0 and 0 V. The AC electrode merely displays rectangular shape CV profiles because of the prominent electric double layer capacitance. Fig. 4b and c reveal galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) curves of AC, which deliver specific capacitances of 285, 262, 252, 230, 225 and 210 F g−1 at current densities of 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, 15 and 20 A g−1, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. 4d, the resistance of AC is 1.75Ω.

Fig. 5 a shows the CV curves of a NiCo2O4@GQDs//AC asymmetric supercapacitor in 2 M KOH aqueous electrolytes at different scan rates within the potential window of 0–1.6 V. And both electrical double-layer capacitance of AC and pseudo-capacitance of NiCo2O4@GQDs contribute to the charge/discharge process shape of the CV curves. The shape of the CV curves does not change obviously as the scan rate increases from 5 to 20 mV s−1, suggesting excellent reversibility and a capacitive behaviour for the electrode reactions over AC electrodes. Fig. 5b displays the GCD curves of the NiCo2O4@GQDs//AC ASC at different current densities ranging from 1 to 30 A g−1. All of them show excellent symmetry for the NiCo2O4 @GQDs//AC device in the potential window of 0–1.6 V. Importantly, the specific capacitance of NiCo2O4@GQDs//AC reaches 107 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. As revealed in Fig. 5c, when the current density increases from 1 to 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 20 and 30 A g−1, the specific capacitance gradually reduces to 107, 103, 100, 95, 91, 87, 82, 77, 70 and 67 F g−1, respectively. The resistance of AC is 1.23Ω (Fig. 5d).

The Ragone plot of the assembled supercapacitor and other devices is shown in Fig. 5e, along with the data of reported supercapacitor devices based on similar materials for comparison. The specific energy density of NiCo2O4@GQDs//AC reaches 38.1 W h kg−1 at a power density of 800 W kg−1, and retains a high value of 25.5 W h kg−1 at a power density of 14.4 kW kg−1, much higher than the previously reported on NiCo2S4/Co9S8//AC (33.5 W h kg−1, 150 W kg−1) [26], Ni2P-150//AC (32.02 W h kg−1, 700 W kg−1) [40], amorphous Ni-P//AC (29.2 W h kg−1, 400 W kg−1) [41], NiCo2O4//AC (14.7 W h kg−1, 175 W kg−1) [11] and Co2P//graphene (24 W h kg−1, 300 W kg−1) [42]. (Table S1).

In addition, long-term cycling stability of an asymmetric supercapacitor device is an important factor to evaluate its electrochemical performance. As shown in Fig. 5f, the supercapacitor device maintains 71.8% of its initial capacitance over 3000 cycles at a current density of 4 A g−1, indicating an excellent capacity retention. Such exceptional performance can be explained by the combination of excellent pseudocapacitive properties of the NiCo2O4@GQDs positive electrode and effective enlargement of potential windows by the AC negative electrode.

The greatly improved pseudocapacitive properties of the NiCo2O4@GQDs can be well explained as follows. First, the GQDs coated on the surface of NiCo2O4 just like pieces of puzzle [38], form highly efficient conductive networks to increase the electronic and ion transmission efficiency, significantly enhancing the electrochemical properties especially rate performance of NiCo2O4@GQDs. Second, the NiCo2O4 has high theoretical capacitance nearly 3000 F g−1 and better conductivity than Co3O4 and NiO [18], which makes it one of the most promising candidates for EES. Third, the tremella-like NiCo2O4 with a mesoporous structure can improve wettability between the material and electrolyte, make the material has good long cycle performance. Furthermore, the high performance of the NiCo2O4@GQDs//AC asymmetric supercapacitor can be attributed to the perfect combination of the positive and negative stabilities of the two electrodes.

In summary, a tremella-like NiCo2O4@ Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) composite has been successfully prepared through a facile post dipping and drying method. NiCo2O4@GQDs displays remarkably high specific capacitance of 1382 F g−1 at a current density of 1.0 A g−1 in 2.0 M KOH electrolytes and excellent cycling stability with a specific capacitance retention of about 99% over 4000 cycles. In addition, an asymmetric supercapacitor based on NiCo2O4@GQDs and AC delivers both a high energy density of 38.1 Wh∙kg−1 (with power density of 800 W kg−1) and an exceptional cycling stability with a capacitance retention of 71.8% after 3000 cycles at the current density of 4.0 A g−1. Therefore, we believe that GQDs will make a great contribution to the electrochemical energy storage in the future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (No. 21801005, U1504218), Key scientific research project plan of Henan Province Colleges and Universities (19A150012, 19A480003) and Start-up funding of Postdoctoral innovation practice base for Anyang Institute of Technology.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2019.01.078.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. Zhang, J. Zhu, Y. Qing, L. Wang, J. Zhao, J. Li, W. Tian, D. Jia, Z. Fan, Ultramicroporous carbons puzzled by graphene quantum dots: integrated high gravimetric, volumetric, and areal capacitances for supercapacitors, Adv. Funct. Mater., 1805898.
